origin: Ancient Christian biblical text, specifically a genealogical sequence counting generations in Jesus’ lineage as presented in the opening chapter of the Gospel of Matthew (Matthew 1). The phrase enumerates three segments: Abraham→David (14), David→Babylonian exile (14), and Babylonian exile→Christ (14), likely to structure the genealogy symbolically.
author: Most likely the author/tradition behind the Gospel of Matthew (commonly attributed to “Matthew,” though scholarly consensus often treats it as written by an unknown early Christian author within the Matthean community).
thoughts: The author seems focused on creating a meaningful, orderly presentation of Jesus’ ancestry—using a symmetrical “14 generations” pattern to convey theological significance (fulfillment of Jewish expectations, divine providence, and an intentional literary structure). There may also be an apologetic motive: to establish legitimacy of Jesus’ messianic claim by rooting him in Israel’s history.
audience: Early Jewish-Christian readers (or a mixed early Christian audience with strong familiarity with Jewish scriptures/history), people who would recognize the significance of Abraham, David, and the Babylonian exile and value genealogical/theological proof of Jesus’ identity.
written_when: Likely in the late 1st century CE, commonly dated around 80–90 CE (with some scholarly discussion spanning a bit earlier/later).
